metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the `current_code_sha` call site: confirm the function signature accepts no `repo_root` argument (it is ContextVar-based), then either update the plan to call `current_code_sha()` without `repo_root` (and document that the ContextVar must be active at emit time) or add a separate `repo_root`-aware SHA accessor.
evidence: Plan quote: 'verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'
Actual signature in src/rebar/llm/config.py line 134: `def current_code_sha() -> str | None:` — no repo_root parameter
The function derives SHA from `current_code_root()` (a ContextVar), not from a repo_root path, so adding `repo_root` is not a simple signature extension — it changes semantics
findings.finding: The plan asserts `build_payload` will stamp `verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)`, treating `current_code_sha` as accepting a `repo_root` argument. The actual implementation is `def current_code_sha() -> str | None:` with no parameters — it derives the SHA from a ContextVar (`current_code_root()`), not from a file-system path. Passing `repo_root` would be a TypeError at runtime.
findings.location: What section: 'stamps two additive top-level fields … verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'
scenarios: At emit time, `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` calls `current_code_sha(repo_root)` → TypeError: current_code_sha() takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given

If the implementer instead calls `current_code_sha()` (no arg), the `repo_root` in the plan spec is misleading and the implementation deviates from the spec — the sidecar-branch's `code_unchanged` check then compares a ContextVar-based SHA against the sidecar's `verified_at_sha` field, which is correct only if the same ContextVar is active at both emit time and re-review time
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)` with `rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha()` in the plan spec, and add a note that the stamped SHA is the active gate snapshot SHA (None in local-mode), matching the existing behavior in `sign_plan_review`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the plan's `current_code_sha` call sites to use `current_code_sha()` (no argument) everywhere — the function is a ContextVar reader, not a `repo_root`-parameterised accessor; the `repo_root` parameter is needed only for `registry_version(repo_root)`, which correctly accepts it.
evidence: Plan text: 'verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'
config.py:134: `def current_code_sha() -> str | None:` — no parameter accepted
attest.py:247: `current_sha = current_code_sha()` — existing callers pass no argument
findings.finding: `current_code_sha` does not accept a `repo_root` argument — the plan specifies calling `rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)` in both `build_payload` and the sidecar branch of `remediation_mode_candidate`, but the function signature is `def current_code_sha() -> str | None` (a ContextVar reader taking no parameters). Passing `repo_root` will raise TypeError at runtime.
findings.location: What section — 'verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)' (appears in both the build_payload stamping spec and the sidecar-branch eligibility spec); corroborated by src/rebar/llm/config.py:134 and existing usage at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:247
scenarios: Implementing `build_payload` as specified raises TypeError when `emit` calls `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` and `build_payload` internally calls `current_code_sha(repo_root)`.
Implementing the sidecar branch of `remediation_mode_candidate` as specified raises TypeError when it calls `current_code_sha(repo_root)` instead of `current_code_sha()`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)` with `rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha()` in both the `build_payload` spec and the sidecar-branch description. `build_payload` still gains `repo_root=None` for `registry_version(repo_root)`, but the SHA is sourced via the ContextVar (no argument).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the `emit()` call-site update in the What section or Scope: `emit(verdict, *, material=None, repo_root=None)` must forward `repo_root` into `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)`.
evidence: Current emit() at sidecar.py:51-69: `payload = build_payload(verdict, material=material)` — no repo_root forwarded
Plan: 'its caller `emit` already holds repo_root and passes it through' — but the current code does NOT pass it
The plan's What section names build_payload's new signature but does not list `emit`'s call-site update as a separate named edit
findings.finding: The plan states `emit` already holds `repo_root` and will pass it through to the newly-added `repo_root` parameter of `build_payload`, but the current `emit` body calls `build_payload(verdict, material=material)` without forwarding `repo_root` — this caller update is a required edit not explicitly enumerated in the plan's edit targets.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:60 (`emit` body) / What section: `build_payload(verdict, *, material=None)` gains a `repo_root=None` keyword
scenarios: If `repo_root` is not forwarded from `emit` into `build_payload`, the new `current_code_sha(repo_root)` call inside `build_payload` will always use the default (None), potentially stamping a wrong SHA in non-default repo-root environments.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'The `emit` function signature already accepts `repo_root`; its internal `build_payload(verdict, material=material)` call must be updated to `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` to forward the context.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the `current_code_sha` call in the `build_payload` spec: drop the `repo_root` argument (it reads from a ContextVar, not a path) — use `current_code_sha()` with no arguments, consistent with every existing call site.
evidence: Plan text: '`verified_at_sha` = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'
src/rebar/llm/config.py:134: `def current_code_sha() -> str | None:` — zero-argument function, reads from `_active_code_root` ContextVar, no `repo_root` parameter
All existing call sites confirm zero-argument form: `attest.py:247: current_sha = current_code_sha()`
findings.finding: `current_code_sha` takes no arguments (reads from a ContextVar), but the plan specifies calling it as `current_code_sha(repo_root)` in `build_payload`. This is an unverified capability assertion that will produce a TypeError at emit time.
findings.location: What section — 'Persist the baseline signals in the sidecar' paragraph: '`verified_at_sha` = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'
scenarios: When `emit` calls the new `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)`, the internal `current_code_sha(repo_root)` call raises TypeError — breaking sidecar emit (best-effort, so silently degrades to `verified_at_sha=None`), meaning every sidecar carries `None` and the sidecar fallback's `code_unchanged` check is always False — the feature never works.
findings.suggested_fix: In `build_payload`, call `current_code_sha()` with no arguments: `verified_at_sha = current_code_sha()`. The ContextVar is already set for the duration of the gate run, so the correct SHA is available without passing `repo_root`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the calling convention for `current_code_sha` in `build_payload`: the function takes no arguments (reads a ContextVar); either call it as `current_code_sha()` (the same pattern as attest.py:247) or explicitly add a `repo_root` overload — update the plan's 'What' section to reflect the actual call signature.
evidence: Plan text: 'verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'
current_code_sha signature at config.py:134: `def current_code_sha() -> str | None:` — no parameters
Existing call at attest.py:247: `current_sha = current_code_sha()` — zero args
Evidence class: Contradicted (the plan asserts a parameter the function does not accept)
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section specifies that `build_payload` (called from `emit`, which holds `repo_root`) will stamp `verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)`, implying `current_code_sha` accepts a `repo_root` argument. But `current_code_sha`'s actual signature is `() -> str | None` (no parameters — it reads from the `_active_code_root` ContextVar); the existing call site in `remediation_mode_candidate` calls it as `current_code_sha()` with no args. Passing `repo_root` to it will raise `TypeError` at runtime.
findings.location: What section — 'build_payload gains a repo_root=None keyword … verified_at_sha = rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha(repo_root)'; also src/rebar/llm/config.py:134
scenarios: build_payload is called from emit() with the new repo_root kwarg forwarded; the internal call to current_code_sha(repo_root) raises TypeError; the except-BLE001 in emit() catches it and logs a warning, so the sidecar emits WITHOUT verified_at_sha (None), silently defeating the sidecar-fallback's code_unchanged check for all reviews.
findings.suggested_fix: Call `current_code_sha()` with no arguments inside `build_payload`, relying on the active ContextVar (the same mechanism already used in `remediation_mode_candidate`). The `repo_root` parameter on `build_payload` is still useful for `registry_version(repo_root)` — just don't forward it to `current_code_sha`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `sidecar.py:emit()`'s internal `build_payload(verdict, material=material)` call at line 61 as a required update site: change it to `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` so the new fields are actually stamped.
evidence: Plan: '`build_payload(verdict, *, material=None)` … gains a `repo_root=None` keyword (its caller `emit` already holds repo_root and passes it through)'
sidecar.py:61: `payload = build_payload(verdict, material=material)` — no repo_root passed today
ABSENCE: the plan never says 'update the call at sidecar.py:61 to pass repo_root=repo_root'
findings.finding: The plan says `build_payload(verdict, *, material=None)` gains a `repo_root=None` keyword and that 'its caller `emit` already holds repo_root and passes it through', but the actual `emit()` call at `sidecar.py:61` is `build_payload(verdict, material=material)` — no `repo_root` argument. The plan identifies the intent but never explicitly names the `emit`-to-`build_payload` internal invocation as a call site requiring update, leaving an implementer to infer it from the prose.
findings.location: What section (sidecar fields paragraph) + sidecar.py:51-69 (emit) + sidecar.py:61
scenarios: An implementer adds `repo_root=None` to `build_payload`'s signature but doesn't update the `emit()` call: `verified_at_sha` and `regver` are stamped as `None` on every sidecar (because `current_code_sha(None)` returns None in local mode), making the sidecar fallback always produce `sidecar_baseline=False` on non-None repo_root environments.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the sidecar fields paragraph: 'The `emit()` internal call at sidecar.py:61 must be updated to `build_payload(verdict, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` to thread the already-available `repo_root` into the payload builder.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC#2 (or add a sibling AC) to explicitly cover sidecar-branch negative cases for registry_unchanged=False, within_window=False, and sidecar_baseline=False (no prior sidecar present), with unit-test oracles for each.
evidence: AC#2 oracle: 'negative-case unit tests in the same module covering both' — 'both' refers only to 'code drifted since the prior sidecar' and 'a prior sidecar missing verified_at_sha or regver'
Sidecar branch reasons dict: {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window} — registry_unchanged=False and within_window=False cases are not named in any AC
ABSENCE: no AC asserts behavior when no prior sidecar exists at all in the sidecar fallback branch (sidecar_baseline=False path)
findings.finding: The sidecar-branch negative test matrix in AC#2 names only two failure modes ('code drifted' and 'missing verified_at_sha or regver') but the new sidecar branch has five reason keys — sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window — leaving registry_unchanged=False, within_window=False, and sidecar_baseline=False (no prior sidecar exists at all) untested for the new branch. These are all new code paths that did not exist before this change.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#2 oracle / What — sidecar branch reason keys
scenarios: Registry version bumped between BLOCK round 1 and round 2 — sidecar branch should return eligible=False but has no test asserting this
Window expired between rounds — sidecar branch should return eligible=False but has no test
No prior sidecar exists at all when the sidecar fallback is attempted — should return ineligible but untested
findings.suggested_fix: Add three named negative unit tests: (a) fixture sidecar present with all fields but registry version changed → eligible=False, baseline='sidecar'; (b) fixture sidecar present with all fields but timestamp outside window → eligible=False, baseline='sidecar'; (c) no prior sidecar file exists → eligible=False, baseline='sidecar', sidecar_baseline=False. Reference these explicitly in AC#2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py` in Scope as requiring updates (at minimum `test_no_signature_not_eligible` and any other tests asserting on `reasons['signed']` must be revised to match the new two-branch reasons-dict shape).
evidence: test_no_signature_not_eligible calls _setup(monkeypatch, signed=False) then asserts d['reasons']['signed'] is False
Plan's sidecar branch reasons dict contains EXACTLY {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window} — no 'signed' key
Plan states: 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline … now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible'
The test is in tests/unit/, which is NOT named for update in the plan's Scope or AC list
findings.finding: The existing unit test `test_no_signature_not_eligible` (and the sidecar-branch fall-through logic for the `signed=False` path) will break under the new two-branch design: it asserts `d['reasons']['signed'] is False`, but the sidecar branch emits NO `signed` key — the assertion will KeyError or silently mismatch.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py:84-88
scenarios: After implementing the two-branch fallthrough, `signed=False` no longer early-returns with `{eligible: False, reasons: {signed: False, …}}`; it enters the sidecar branch and returns `{eligible: …, reasons: {sidecar_baseline: …, …}}`, so `d['reasons']['signed']` raises KeyError.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `## Scope` IN list: 'update tests/unit/test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py: revise tests that assert on reasons["signed"] for the signed=False path to instead assert on the sidecar-branch reasons shape.'

findings.finding: AC1's proving command (`-k "remediation"`) is a substring filter that can silently include unrelated tests or exclude new tests whose names don't contain 'remediation', making the oracle non-deterministic with respect to the specific new fixture.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 oracle: '`python -m pytest tests/unit -k "remediation" -x`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_remediation_mode_candidate.py -x` (or the full node-id of the new positive-case test) to ensure the oracle is both necessary and sufficient.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `grep -qi "sidecar fallback" docs/plan-review-gate.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a consumer protocol for the two-shape `reasons` dict: either (a) specify that ALL consumers MUST first check `baseline` before reading branch-specific keys, or (b) standardize a union key set with `None`/absent sentinels for keys not applicable to a given branch, and call this out in the Scope or Acceptance Criteria.
evidence: Plan: 'the sidecar branch's reasons dict contains EXACTLY five keys - {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window} - where `sidecar_baseline` is True when a prior sidecar … exists; there is no `signed` and no separate `prior_sidecar` key in that branch'
Plan: 'Decision-dict shape per branch: the returned dict gains `baseline: "signature" | "sidecar"'
attest.py:214-221: current `reasons` is initialized with all six signature-branch keys unconditionally
ABSENCE: no protocol described for how downstream consumers of `reasons` (tests, observability) handle two different key vocabularies on the same dict type
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the sidecar branch's `reasons` dict contains EXACTLY five keys (`{sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window}`) while the signature branch keeps its six existing keys (`{signed, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, prior_sidecar, within_window}`). No protocol is specified for callers or tests that enumerate `reasons` keys — the decision dict is a shared output schema, and downstream callers expecting a stable key set (e.g., the integration test AC asserting `coverage.remediation.eligible`) may receive a structurally different dict depending on which branch ran, with no discriminator until `baseline` is read.
findings.location: What section (Rekey eligibility paragraph, 'sidecar branch's reasons dict contains EXACTLY five keys') + attest.py:214-221 (current reasons init)
scenarios: An integration test or offline consumer that iterates `reasons.items()` or checks for the `signed` key will silently misread a sidecar-branch decision dict (no `signed` key present).
A log/observability consumer that records all `reasons` keys for the signature branch will produce sparse/incorrect records for sidecar-branch decisions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which timestamp is used for `within_window` in the sidecar branch: state explicitly whether it is `latest_review_timestamp()` (the newest-file filename prefix, matching the signature branch) or the baseline sidecar's own file timestamp, and note how the two diverge when the newest file is unreadable.
evidence: Plan: 'sidecar branch's reasons dict contains EXACTLY five keys - {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window}'
sidecar.py:247-250: `latest_review_timestamp` reads the newest FILE's timestamp (no JSON parse, by filename prefix), not the newest usable payload's timestamp
sidecar.py:154: `latest_review_result` walks back from newest to oldest for the usable baseline
ABSENCE: the plan never says 'within_window in the sidecar branch uses latest_review_timestamp() (the newest-file timestamp), not the baseline sidecar's own file timestamp'
findings.finding: The sidecar branch lists `within_window` as one of its five reason keys but the plan does not specify which timestamp is used as the measurement basis. The signature branch uses `latest_review_timestamp()` (the newest sidecar file's filename prefix — newest-file timestamp). In the sidecar fallback, the baseline is the most recent USABLE sidecar (walk-back past garbled files), whose file timestamp may differ from the newest-file timestamp when the newest sidecar is corrupt. The plan is silent on whether `within_window` in the sidecar branch uses the baseline-sidecar's timestamp or the newest-file timestamp.
findings.location: What section (sidecar branch's reasons dict, 'within_window') + sidecar.py:226-257 (latest_review_timestamp) + sidecar.py:118-174 (latest_review_result walk-back)
scenarios: Newest sidecar file is garbled (mid-emit crash); `latest_review_timestamp()` returns its (recent) timestamp; `latest_review_result()` walk-back returns an older valid sidecar as the baseline. `within_window` is True (measured from the garbled newest file) but the baseline sidecar is old — semantically inconsistent.
Implementer uses the baseline sidecar's own timestamp for `within_window` — measurement diverges from the signature branch's behavior, making tests comparing the two paths fragile.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the sidecar branch description: 'within_window uses `latest_review_timestamp()` (the newest-file filename prefix) identically to the signature branch — consistent even when the newest file is garbled, since the window measures the last review ATTEMPT, not the last usable review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `sidecar_baseline` treats a `None` value for any of the three fields as 'missing' (sidecar_baseline=False), AND that `code_unchanged` in the sidecar branch mirrors the existing signature-path guard: `bool(sidecar_sha) and sidecar_sha == current_code_sha()` — so a local-mode sidecar (verified_at_sha=None) always yields sidecar_baseline=False rather than accidentally satisfying code_unchanged via None==None.
evidence: config.py:134-146: `current_code_sha()` returns None in local mode (no attested snapshot)
Plan text: 'Sourcing failures stamp None (never raise)' — so None is a valid stamp value
Plan text: 'A sidecar missing any baseline field leaves that precondition False' — 'missing' is ambiguous: does None count as missing?
Existing signature branch (attest.py:248): uses `bool(signed_sha) and signed_sha == current_sha` to reject None explicitly — no equivalent guard specified for the sidecar branch
findings.finding: The sidecar branch's `code_unchanged` comparison semantics are unspecified for the local-mode case where `current_code_sha()` returns `None` AND the sidecar's `verified_at_sha` is `None` (stamped as None by a local-mode prior review). A naive `current == sidecar_sha` evaluates `None == None = True`, making `code_unchanged=True` in local mode — where SHA-based code-drift detection is explicitly unreliable. The plan's 'sidecar_baseline=True requires all three fields present' rule would prevent eligibility only if `None` is treated as absent; the plan does not define 'present' precisely enough to exclude `None`.
findings.location: What section — sidecar fallback branch: 'code_unchanged compares current_code_sha() against the sidecar's verified_at_sha'; 'sidecar_baseline is True when a prior sidecar carrying all three baseline fields (material_fingerprint, verified_at_sha, regver) exists'
scenarios: A local-mode BLOCK stamps verified_at_sha=None; a local-mode re-review finds verified_at_sha=None in the sidecar; if sidecar_baseline treats None as 'present', sidecar_baseline=True and code_unchanged = (None == None) = True — incorrectly granting eligibility in an environment where SHA-based code-drift detection is meaningless.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the sidecar-branch spec: 'sidecar_baseline is True when all three fields are present AND non-None; a None value for any field is treated as absent.' For code_unchanged: 'mirrors the existing signature-path guard: `bool(sidecar_sha) and sidecar_sha == current_code_sha()`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit AC (or extend existing ones) that directly asserts `build_payload` output contains `verified_at_sha` and `regver` keys on both PASS and BLOCK verdict fixtures, including a case where the sourcing call returns None and the downstream eligibility correctly treats it as missing.
evidence: No AC asserts 'build_payload output contains verified_at_sha and regver' or tests that emit actually writes these fields on a PASS verdict sidecar.
AC4 provides indirect integration coverage (BLOCK loop reaching eligibility TRUE implies the fields were written by a prior PASS or BLOCK round), but a unit-level producing assertion is absent.

The What section explicitly describes stamping as 'ALWAYS self-sourced at emit time... present identically on PASS and BLOCK verdicts' — this is a distinct testable property with no covering criterion.
findings.finding: The deliverable 'Persist the baseline signals in the sidecar' (build_payload stamps verified_at_sha and regver on every verdict) has no AC that directly verifies these fields are written into the sidecar payload on PASS verdicts — only the consumer (eligibility) is tested, not the producer.
findings.location: What section — first bullet ('Persist the baseline signals'); AC1–AC4
scenarios: A mis-wired implementation writes the fields only on PASS (not BLOCK) or vice versa; AC4 passes only if the prior round happened to be the covered type, masking the bug.
A sourcing failure that silently stamps None instead of aborting is specified as acceptable, but no AC verifies the None-stamping behavior triggers the fail-safe downstream.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Unit test: call `build_payload(verdict="BLOCK", ...)` / `build_payload(verdict="PASS", ...)` with a stubbed `current_code_sha` and registry accessor; assert top-level keys `verified_at_sha` and `regver` are present; also assert that None values leave sidecar_baseline False in the eligibility function.'

findings.finding: The What section describes that `build_payload` in sidecar.py gains two new fields (`verified_at_sha`, `regver`) — a concrete deliverable — but no AC directly verifies that these fields are actually emitted by `build_payload`. ACs 1 and 2 consume these fields as fixture inputs to `remediation_mode_candidate`, not as outputs of `build_payload`, leaving the emit side untested by any named oracle.
findings.location: What section ('build_payload … gains two additive optional fields') vs AC1, AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `build_payload` emits `verified_at_sha` (current code SHA) and `regver` (registry version) when available. Oracle: unit test calling `build_payload(...)` with a mock SHA/regver source and asserting the returned dict contains both fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback/back-out path for the two error-path fallthrough changes (e.g., a config flag that reverts both paths to 'return ineligible', or an expand-contract sequence where the fallthrough is gated behind an opt-in flag first), so operators can recover without a code deploy if false-positive eligibility is observed in production.
evidence: 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline — a signature verification error AND a successful verification whose manifest is not a plan-review manifest (the early return at attest.py:231) — now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible' — this is an explicit behavioral change for existing code paths with observable production impact.
No rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequencing is described anywhere in the plan for these path changes.
The plan describes fail-safe behavior for data-layer gaps (missing fields → ineligible) but not for the code-path routing change itself.
findings.finding: The behavioral change to two previously-ineligible code paths (signature verification error; successful verification of a non-plan-review manifest) — now falling through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible — has no described rollback mechanism or feature flag if this change produces incorrect eligibility (false positives) in production.
findings.location: What section — 'BOTH paths that today yield no usable signature baseline... now fall through to the sidecar branch instead of returning ineligible'
scenarios: A bug in the sidecar fallback causes the floor to engage when it should not (false positive eligibility), suppressing advisories that should appear; with no flag or rollback path, the only recovery is a hot-patch deploy.
A signature verification library regression causes all reviews to fall through to the sidecar branch, silently degrading the signature check to a sidecar check without operator visibility.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Rollback subsection under Scope or a new 'Rollback path' field: 'A single env-var / config flag `REBAR_SIDECAR_FALLBACK_ENABLED` (default: true after this task ships) can be set to false to restore the pre-task behavior of returning ineligible on both error paths, enabling zero-deploy rollback if production false positives are detected.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the rationale for the two-branch eligibility trust hierarchy (signature > sidecar), why sidecar fields are trusted as baseline, and the fail-safe invariant (missing fields → ineligible), so the architectural decision is recoverable by future maintainers.
evidence: Plan says: 'Docs: docs/plan-review-gate.md's eligibility description updated (replacing the honest-limitation paragraph added by task 67da of this epic)'
No ADR or CLAUDE.md update is mentioned anywhere in the plan
The sidecar is now a trust source for security-adjacent eligibility gating — a meaningful architectural trust-chain change
T5e criterion (c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — elevating the sidecar to a trusted baseline source for eligibility decisions, establishing a two-branch trust hierarchy (signature > sidecar) — but only updates docs/plan-review-gate.md's behavioral description, with no ADR or design-doc capturing the rationale, alternatives considered, and fail-safe invariants.
findings.location: Docs section; What section (two-branch eligibility rekey)
scenarios: A future maintainer sees the two-branch logic in attest.py, doesn't understand why sidecar is trusted as a baseline, and either breaks the precedence invariant or weakens the fail-safe
The rationale for choosing sidecar-as-baseline over alternatives (e.g., storing baseline in a separate config, replaying signature) is lost, making the decision hard to revisit
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., docs/adr/0NNN-sidecar-eligibility-fallback.md) or a CLAUDE.md section covering: (1) why sidecar is an acceptable trusted baseline when no signature exists, (2) the precedence rule and why it's safe, (3) the fail-safe invariant and its rationale. The plan-review-gate.md update covers behavioral description; the ADR covers the 'why this design' question.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one observable signal (log line at INFO/WARNING level, or a metric increment) for (a) the sidecar fallback branch being entered, and (b) each None-stamping event in build_payload when current_code_sha or regver sourcing fails, so operators can detect silent degradation.
evidence: Plan says 'Sourcing failures stamp None (never raise; emit stays best-effort)' but specifies no logging of that failure
Decision-dict gains baseline: 'signature' | 'sidecar' but only if an operator actively reads the sidecar JSON — no metric or log line is mentioned

The plan names no alerting or log emission for the new fallback branch being entered, so a deployment where 100% of reviews silently fall back to sidecar (e.g., signature infra broken) is indistinguishable from intended operation
findings.finding: The new sidecar fallback path and the baseline-field stamping failures (None-stamped verified_at_sha / regver) are not instrumented with any log, metric, or trace, making it invisible to operators whether the system is using the signature path vs. the sidecar fallback, or whether baseline fields are silently failing to populate at emit time.
findings.location: What section — two-branch eligibility rekey; sidecar.py build_payload instrumentation
scenarios: Signature verification infra breaks silently → all reviews fall through to sidecar fallback → operators see no signal
git/registry accessor fails repeatedly → verified_at_sha/regver stamped as None on every emit → floor permanently ineligible via sidecar branch → no metric fires
findings.suggested_fix: In build_payload, emit a structured log warning (e.g., logger.warning('plan_review.sidecar.baseline_field_missing', field='verified_at_sha', reason=str(exc))) when each sourcing call fails and stamps None. In remediation_mode_candidate, emit a debug/info log line when the sidecar branch is entered (e.g., logger.info('plan_review.eligibility.baseline', path='sidecar')). These are low-overhead and make the two new failure modes observable without changing fail-safe semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py` to the Scope's IN list as a new file to be created (it does not exist and must be authored as part of this ticket).
evidence: Directory listing of tests/interfaces/lifecycle/ shows no test_block_loop_remediation.py
Search for 'test_block_loop_remediation' returns zero hits across the entire repo
AC#4 asserts this file as an oracle: 'Oracle: `python -m pytest tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py -x` green'
findings.finding: The integration test file `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py` named as the AC#4 oracle does not exist in the repository.
findings.location: AC#4: `python -m pytest tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py -x`
scenarios: `make test` passes all existing tests but AC#4's oracle command exits non-zero (file not found), leaving the BLOCK-loop integration coverage unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Under `## Scope`, change the unit-test line to: `IN: two sidecar fields, the two-branch eligibility rekey, docs update, unit tests over the eligibility decision matrix, one integration test over a BLOCK loop (new file: tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_block_loop_remediation.py). OUT: …`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification in the plan (or a referenced ADR) explaining why the unsigned sidecar integrity level is acceptable for the sidecar-fallback eligibility path — or consider whether emitting a signed BLOCK receipt would let the existing signature path cover BLOCK loops without a trust-level change.
evidence: Plan text: 'SIDECAR FALLBACK only when no valid plan-review manifest exists — baseline = the most recent REVIEW_RESULT sidecar'
sidecar.py docstring: 'It is a reducer-ignored sidecar … best-effort observability — a failed emit must NEVER fail the review'

The sidecar has no cryptographic integrity guarantee — any process with write access to the tracker dir can forge or mutate a sidecar; the plan does not acknowledge this or argue it is acceptable for this use case
No discussion of alternative: e.g., emitting a lightweight signed record on BLOCK so a subsequent review can use the signature path unchanged
findings.finding: The plan introduces an unsigned observability sidecar (best-effort, no HMAC/signature) as the trust anchor for a security-adjacent eligibility gate, but does not justify this trust-level tradeoff against the obvious alternative of signing BLOCK verdicts (a lightweight signed BLOCK receipt), nor against other established approaches. This is a novel architectural pattern with no prior-art comparison.
findings.location: What section — 'Rekey eligibility in remediation_mode_candidate with explicit two-branch control flow: (2) SIDECAR FALLBACK'
scenarios: An attacker (or a bug) that can write to the tracker dir can craft a sidecar with arbitrary `material_fingerprint`, `verified_at_sha`, and `regver`, causing a plan that actually changed code or registry to appear eligible for the floor — bypassing a full review.
Alternatively: a legitimate BLOCK-loop install where the tracker dir is on a shared filesystem may hit stale-read races between sidecar write and next read.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback schema-state assertion to the plan: state that if the sidecar-branch code is reverted, sidecars already carrying `verified_at_sha`/`regver` are harmless inert JSON, the rolled-back `remediation_mode_candidate` reverts to signature-only behavior, and no sidecar files need to be mutated or removed.
evidence: Plan states: 'Both fields are additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema; older sidecars without them simply fail the fallback preconditions (fail-safe).' — addresses forward-compat but not the post-rollback state.
T11 rubric: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 — a rollback can exit 0 while leaving the schema in an incorrect intermediate state.'

ABSENCE: no explicit statement that rolled-back code (which loses the sidecar branch) treats existing sidecars with the new fields as harmless inert JSON, restoring exactly today's signature-only behavior.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly assert the schema state AFTER a code rollback — it says the new fields are 'additive-optional' and that 'older sidecars without them simply fail the fallback preconditions,' but never states positively what the system state looks like if the sidecar-branch code is reverted while some sidecars already carry verified_at_sha/regver.
findings.location: What section — sidecar field description: 'Both fields are additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema; older sidecars without them simply fail the fallback preconditions (fail-safe).'
scenarios: Code is rolled back after some BLOCK-verdict sidecars have been emitted with verified_at_sha/regver. The rolled-back remediation_mode_candidate lacks the sidecar branch entirely. The extra fields in existing sidecar files are unread. Without an explicit assertion in the plan, reviewers cannot confirm this is intentionally safe vs. an unanalyzed edge case.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section (sidecar field description), append: 'Post-rollback state: if this change is reverted, the sidecar branch is removed from remediation_mode_candidate and the system reverts to today's signature-only behavior; existing sidecars carrying verified_at_sha/regver are unread but harmless — they are inert extra JSON fields in the v2 payload and require no cleanup.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the What section whether `build_payload`'s `schema` string stays `"plan_review_result_v2"` (and the schema guard's allowlist is unchanged) or gets bumped to `v3` (requiring the guard to be updated accordingly).
evidence: Plan: 'Both fields are additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema; older sidecars without them simply fail the fallback preconditions (fail-safe).'
sidecar.py:429: `"schema": "plan_review_result_v2"` — the current schema string is hardcoded
sidecar.py:162-165: schema guard accepts only `plan_review_result_v1` / `plan_review_result_v2`
ABSENCE: the plan never says 'schema string stays plan_review_result_v2' or 'bump to v3'
findings.finding: The plan adds `verified_at_sha` and `regver` as new top-level fields to `build_payload` and calls them 'additive-optional on the existing plan-review sidecar schema', but never states whether the `schema` string in the payload stays `"plan_review_result_v2"` or gets bumped. The existing `latest_review_result` schema guard (sidecar.py:162-165) admits only `plan_review_result_v1` and `plan_review_result_v2`; if the schema string changes, the guard silently rejects every new sidecar, and the sidecar fallback can never fire.
findings.location: What section (sidecar fields paragraph) + sidecar.py:162-165 + sidecar.py:429
scenarios: If an implementer bumps the schema string to v3 (reasonable given the field additions), latest_review_result skips every new sidecar, the sidecar fallback always returns sidecar_baseline=False, and BLOCK loops never become eligible — silently.

If schema stays v2, the guard still passes, but there is no machine-readable way to distinguish 'v2 with baseline fields' from 'v2 without them' — which is precisely what the sidecar_baseline check relies on.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the sidecar fields paragraph: 'The `schema` field value stays `"plan_review_result_v2"` (no bump); the new fields are additive on the existing schema string, so `latest_review_result`'s allowlist requires no change.' This removes implementer ambiguity and closes the silent breakage path.

findings.finding: AC5's negative-case doc oracle (`! grep -q "requires a prior certified PASS signature" docs/plan-review-gate.md`) is trivially passing today and will continue to trivially pass after any doc update because the literal string `"requires a prior certified PASS signature"` does not appear in the raw file — the current text at line 337 uses markdown bold (`**certified PASS**`) without the word 'requires'. The oracle cannot verify the limitation paragraph was actually removed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / AC5 / `! grep -q "requires a prior certified PASS signature" docs/plan-review-gate.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the negative oracle with: `! grep -q "Known limitation — the floor does not engage during BLOCK loops" docs/plan-review-gate.md`

findings.finding: The plan under-specifies HOW `verified_at_sha` and `regver` reach `build_payload` on a BLOCK verdict — whether self-sourced inside `build_payload` or injected by the caller — creating a risk the implementer hooks them only on the PASS signing path and omits them from the BLOCK sidecar-emit path, making the sidecar fallback structurally inert for the exact scenario the plan targets.
findings.location: What section — 'build_payload in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py gains two additive optional fields - verified_at_sha … falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`build_payload` self-sources `verified_at_sha` via `rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha()` and `regver` via `registry_version(repo_root)` at emit time — both calls happen unconditionally (PASS and BLOCK alike) because the ContextVar `_active_code_root` is active throughout `_run_plan_review` in attested mode. The `repo_root` argument must therefore be threaded into `build_payload` (or into `sidecar.emit`) to allow the `registry_version` call.'

findings.finding: `verified_at_sha` placement in `build_payload` is ambiguous: the plan says it is 'sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit', but the orchestrator currently stamps `verified_at_sha` only on the SIGNATURE MANIFEST (`attest.sign_plan_review`, line 114), not on the verdict dict. `build_payload(verdict, *, material=None)` therefore cannot read it from the verdict. No call site is specified where the orchestrator stamps `verified_at_sha` onto the verdict before `sidecar.emit` is called, and `build_payload`'s signature does not include a `verified_at_sha` parameter.
findings.location: What section / sidecar.py build_payload / attest.py:114
findings.suggested_fix: Add `verified_at_sha: str | None = None` to `build_payload`'s signature, sourced by the caller: in `sidecar.emit`, pass `verified_at_sha=current_code_sha()` (mirroring how `material` is passed). The 'falls back to `current_code_sha()` at emit' phrasing already implies the emit call site, so the fallback is emit-time not build-time.

findings.finding: `regver` placement in `build_payload` is ambiguous: the plan adds `regver` (criteria-registry version) as an optional field in `build_payload`, but `build_payload` currently has no access to `registry_version()` (which requires `repo_root`), and neither `sidecar.emit` nor `build_payload` currently accept `repo_root` as a parameter. No call site or parameter-passing path is specified.
findings.location: What section / sidecar.py build_payload / sidecar.emit

findings.finding: The plan asserts verified_at_sha for build_payload is 'sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path' — but gate_source.annotate_result (the only place that stamps verdict['verified_at_sha']) is called at __init__.py:406 AFTER _run_plan_review returns, while sidecar.emit is called at __init__.py:537-538 INSIDE _run_plan_review. The verdict dict passed to sidecar.emit therefore does NOT carry verified_at_sha from annotate_result at emit time.
findings.location: What section / 'Persist the baseline signals in the sidecar' paragraph; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:396-406 vs 537-538
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 'What' description to: 'verified_at_sha is sourced from rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit time (the ContextVar set by gate_read_root is still active when sidecar.emit runs inside _run_plan_review, so the value is identical to what the manifest later records; it is None in local mode).'

findings.finding: The plan describes 'the existing early-return on signature-verification errors' being replaced by fall-through to the sidecar branch. But the actual early-return at attest.py:231 (`if not is_plan_review_manifest(manifest): return ...`) fires after a *successful* `verify_signature` call that returned no plan-review manifest (the BLOCK outcome) — not on a signature-verification error. The current broad `except Exception` handler (line 277) is the actual error guard. The plan mischaracterizes the control flow being modified, which may confuse the implementer about which return site to change.
findings.location: What section / 'The existing early-return on signature-verification errors is replaced by fall-through to the sidecar branch'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The existing early-return on signature-verification errors is replaced by fall-through to the sidecar branch' with 'The existing early-return `if not is_plan_review_manifest(manifest): return {"eligible": False, ...}` (attest.py:231) is replaced by fall-through to the sidecar branch when no valid plan-review manifest exists.'

findings.finding: The plan's sidecar-branch `eligible` computation is underspecified with respect to the existing `prior_sidecar` reason key. The plan says the sidecar branch replaces `signed` with `sidecar_baseline` and computes `eligible` as `all()` over `{sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window}` — but the current `reasons` dict also contains `prior_sidecar`. In a BLOCK loop there is no signature, so the sidecar branch fires; but `prior_sidecar` will be `False` (it was set under the now-fall-through signature path and never updated in the sidecar branch). If `eligible = all(reasons.values())` still includes `prior_sidecar`, the sidecar branch is ineligible in the BLOCK-loop case this task is meant to fix. The plan is silent on whether `prior_sidecar` is removed from the reasons dict, set to `True` unconditionally in the sidecar branch, or simply excluded from the `all()` predicate.
findings.location: What section: 'the sidecar branch replaces `signed` with `sidecar_baseline` … and computes eligible over {sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window}'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:214-283
findings.suggested_fix: Fold `prior_sidecar` into `sidecar_baseline`: `sidecar_baseline` is True when a prior sidecar carrying all three baseline fields exists — which already implies a prior sidecar exists. Remove `prior_sidecar` from the sidecar-branch reasons dict (or keep it and set it True alongside sidecar_baseline when the sidecar has finding text). Document that the sidecar-branch reasons dict shape is `{sidecar_baseline, plan_changed, code_unchanged, registry_unchanged, within_window}` with no `prior_sidecar` key, so `all(reasons.values())` is correct by construction.

findings.finding: The plan's stated two-source resolution for `verified_at_sha` in `build_payload` — 'sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to `current_code_sha()` at emit' — describes a preferred-source branch that is structurally unreachable: `sidecar.emit()` is called at line 538 of `__init__.py` BEFORE `sign_plan_review()` at line 554, so the attestation-path value never exists at emit time. An implementer following this spec literally would write a preferred-source lookup that never fires, adding dead code and obscuring the true sourcing.
findings.location: What section — 'Persist the baseline signals in the sidecar: `build_payload` … `verified_at_sha` (the review-time code SHA, sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit' with: 'stamped onto the verdict dict (e.g., `verdict["verified_at_sha"] = current_code_sha()`) immediately before `sidecar.emit()` is called — analogous to how `material_fingerprint` is set on the verdict at line 510 — so `build_payload` reads it from the verdict dict.' This keeps the sourcing deterministic, avoids a dead fallback branch, and mirrors the existing material-fingerprint pattern exactly.

findings.finding: The plan claims `verified_at_sha` in `build_payload` will be 'sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit'. But `sidecar.emit` is called at `__init__.py:538`, while `sign_plan_review` (which stamps `verified_at_sha` onto the manifest) runs at line 554 — AFTER the sidecar emit. The verdict dict at emit time never carries a `verified_at_sha` key from the signing path. The 'same value the orchestrator stamps' primary source does not exist in the verdict at emit time; `current_code_sha()` is the only viable source.
findings.location: What section / 'verified_at_sha (the review-time code SHA, sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'sourced from the same value the orchestrator stamps on the verdict/attestation path, falling back to rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit' with 'sourced from rebar.llm.config.current_code_sha() at emit time (sidecar.emit runs before sign_plan_review, so the signed manifest's verified_at_sha is not yet available in the verdict dict)'

findings.finding: The `reasons` dict shape for the two branches is underspecified: the plan says the sidecar branch replaces `signed` with `sidecar_baseline` and `eligible` is computed over only that branch's keys — but it does not specify whether the returned `reasons` dict is branch-specific (only contains the active branch's keys) or still initializes with all keys (inactive branch's keys default to `False`). The existing code initializes `reasons` with all six keys statically before branching. A consumer testing `reasons['signed']` on a sidecar-branch result would get `False` (the initialized default) rather than `KeyError`, silently misrepresenting the branch taken.
findings.location: What section / attest.py:214-220 (reasons dict initialization)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
